summary: Gruber argues that the iPhone 15 Pro overheating controversy is a nothingburger, countering Ming-Chi Kuo's claim that the titanium frame caused thermal issues. Kuo's post blamed the new frame design and predicted Apple would need to throttle performance, which drove Apple's stock price down. Apple issued a statement attributing the warmth to post-setup background activity, an iOS 17 bug, and third-party app issues like Instagram and Uber. Gruber reports that Apple told him the titanium frame actually dissipates heat better than the previous stainless steel frames. He argues Kuo's post was likely a TSMC-sourced leak deflecting blame from their 3nm process, with the titanium frame blame being Kuo's own unfounded speculation.
key_points: Apple says iPhone 15 Pro overheating stems from iOS 17 bugs, post-setup background activity, and third-party app bugs — not hardware design flaws
The titanium frame actually improves heat dissipation compared to previous stainless steel frames, and Apple has no plans to throttle the A17 chip
Instagram had a bug consuming 1% battery per minute while idle, causing identical overheating on both iPhone 15 Pro and iPhone 14 Pro — proving it wasn't a new hardware issue
Kuo's report was likely sourced from TSMC deflecting blame from their 3nm process, with the titanium frame claim being Kuo's own speculation
Gruber questions why iOS allows any app to consume enough resources to make a device too hot to hold comfortably
money_quotes: Not only is the titanium frame of the iPhone 15 Pro models not an issue, cooling-wise, but Apple told me — and I have no reason to doubt — that the iPhone 15 Pro and Pro Max are better at heat dissipation than any previous iPhone that used a stainless steel frame.

There is absolutely nothing wrong — and in fact much that is good — with the heat dissipation of the titanium frame, and Apple has no plan to in any way throttle the A17's performance. They have bugs to fix in iOS 17, that is all.
Real shocker that two apps made with a Frankensteinian mishmash of web and native UI toolkits would run amok, resource-wise.
His post on this overheating issue is almost transparently a leak from TSMC — a sort of 'Whatever is going on with the iPhone 15 Pro heat dissipation, it has nothing to do with our 3nm process'.
Blaming it on the new titanium frame was just wild speculation on Kuo's part, and by all evidence is completely wrong.
Kuo's influence stems not from his intuition but from his sources, but that's the problem with an allegation like this one.
If Kuo has any respect for accuracy or truth, as I'm sure he does, he'll issue a full retraction.
key_insight: The iPhone 15 Pro overheating was caused by software bugs in iOS 17 and third-party apps, not the titanium hardware design, and Kuo's influential but wrong report was likely TSMC spin that he embellished with unfounded speculation.
